informal: A continuous, strictly increasing function $\mu \colon (0, \infty) \rightarrow (0, \infty)$ is a modulus of continuity if $\mu(s) \rightarrow 0$ as $s \rightarrow 0$. A function $f \colon [a, b] \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ has modulus of continuity $\mu$ if $|f(s) - f(t)| \leq \mu(|s - t|)$ for all $s, t \in [a, b]$. Prove that a function is uniformly continuous if and only if it has a modulus of continuity.
lean:
theorem Pugh_exercise_4_15a (a b : ℝ) (hab : a ≤ b) (f : Icc a b → ℝ) :
  UniformContinuous f ↔ ∃ (μ : ℝ → ℝ), ∀ (x : ℝ), (x > 0 → μ x > 0) ∧
  StrictMonoOn μ (Ioi 0) ∧ Tendsto μ (𝓝 0) (𝓝 0) ∧
  (∀ s t : Icc a b, |f s - f t| ≤ μ (|s - t|))
reasoning: The informal definition of "modulus of continuity" requires μ to be continuous on (0,∞), but the Lean signature only encodes strict monotonicity on (0,∞), positivity, and Tendsto at 0 — it omits continuity on (0,∞) entirely (strict monotonicity does not imply continuity). This makes the existential RHS strictly weaker than the informal one. As a result, the forward direction (UC → ∃μ) of Lean is weaker than the informal one, while the backward direction (∃μ → UC) of Lean is stronger (concludes UC from a weaker hypothesis). Since one direction is weaker and the other is stronger, the overall iff in Lean is not comparable to the informal iff.
